author: @AgnesCongdon-xk8hr
text: I thought it was an episode of the RED-GREEN SHOW. Sorry.  Thanks.

replies.author: @AgnesCongdon-xk8hr
replies.text: Try this color in your note 📝 pad application: FFFFFF or EEEEEE, unless you prefer 0000FF OR 00030A.

author: @AdricM
text: Sorry if this was explained in your other video,  why did you use the extra berrings on the wheels?

author: @karlmyers6518
text: Great little video Luke. That's a decent sized gear. I look forward to the finished project

author: @William_Hada
text: This is a nice design because it is so light in weight compared to an all metal gear of that size.

author: @roberthopgood1894
text:
it will be true ONLY if the wheel is true
but most bike wheels can be trued

author: @sepgorut2492
text: Why are there two video 1077s? This is wrecking havoc with my administration system!

author: @chuxxsss
text: Good work Luke, have a wonderful day.

author: @benniedonald
text: Luke has come a long way from his first video. This could be a great temporary bicycle tire fix. Just keep one handy to switch out until you can get the other repaired or replaced. I plan to make a few gears soon. So this will save me a lot of time. Thanks very well done.

author: @undernetjack
text: Did Rob catch the glue the belt applied to the desk 2:08 as you put on the belt? 😂

author: @ajw6715
text: Very good and if one wanted to make a stronger gear one could use auto body filler to fill the wheel.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Yes, what an awesome idea! If it did fail on the amount of load, I think I will use auto body filler or fiberglass filler instead of expanding foam, that should sort any issues! Thanks for the suggestion mate! :)

author: @johnsshed995
text: The question everyone's thinking,but no one wants to sound stupid asking it is ...How the hell did you stick that belt onto the rim with superglue and not get your fingers attached to the finished object ? lol

replies.author: @electronresonator8882
replies.text: my own experience, I only apply the glue on the middle of the tape, and carefully attach it to the surface, the glue won't dry in 1 second, so I make it flat by pushing gently on the other side of the tape

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: LOL, the truth is I usually get it everywhere hahaha! I don't know why it went so well maybe I'm now a "professional" LOL. Thanks mate! :)

author: @MarioAbbruscato
text: t's a nice, practical idea. Due to the low resistance of the polystyrene I doubt it can withstand a load greater than a few 100 grams. But I could never have thought of a wood pulp and vinyl glue to do the same but a little more resistant (perhaps). Thanks for your interesting and inspiring work. You are strong.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Thank you mate! I might do a bit of an update using body filler/fiberglass filler instead of the expanding foam, I think that would be able to handle a greater load. I also like the idea of using wood pulp and vinyl glue that would be awesome! Thanks for the suggestion mate! :)

replies.author: @harleyson4516
replies.text: Paper mache might be sufficient, if messy. I would try titebond III wood glue. It's waterproof and rated for at least 3,000psi (~20,000kpa) I think.

replies.author: @benniedonald
replies.text: It all depends on your needs. Are you driving industrial conveyors? Maybe you just need a gear for a small project. 🤔

author: @lylehintz7275
text: Nice presentation and idea. How do you get the end of the cable to end on a full tooth?

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: I offered up the belt a few times and marked it where it lined up, I cut along the mark so when I wrapped it around the rim the 2 ends were just touching. Then when I glued it they lined up perfectly, I thought that was pretty cool! Thank you mate! :)

author: @lylehintz7275
text: Nice idea, how do you get the cable to end on a full tooth?

replies.author: @eriknielsen1849
replies.text: Yes bit of a mistory for me too

replies.author: @mikebond6328
replies.text: I think the solution was a scarf joint. Rob did a video on how to do it a while back.

author: @AtlasReburdened
text: I feel like there might be problems with the foam collapsing under the repetitive loading of being used as a gear.

replies.author: @AtlasReburdened
replies.text: @scotttovey Closed cell foam also experiences inelastic deformation under prolonged cyclical loading.

replies.author: @AtlasReburdened
replies.text: @scotttovey I think that would work. Most rubber hits it's yield point before inelastically deforming.

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
@AtlasReburdened 
You can also get fiber and build it up layer by layer using rubber or a resin to bond it together like fiberglass.
